query: trends in breast cancer survival rates over time

retrieved_docs.title: Breast Cancer -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: A rapid increase in the incidence of breast cancer was noted until 2000, after which the incidence began to decline. More significant decreases occur in women younger than 50 years. With early detection and significant advances in treatment, breast cancer death rates have decreased over the past 25 years in North America and parts of Europe. In the US, breast cancer-related mortality dropped by 43% from 1980 to 2020. However, in many African and Asian countries (eg, Uganda, South Korea, and India), breast cancer incidence and death rates continue to rise. [6] Even within the US, marked disparity exists in detection and survival rates based on socioeconomic status and race. Although the incidence is highest among non-Hispanic whites, the mortality rate is significantly higher among African Americans. According to the American Cancer Society (ACS), breast cancer rates among women from various racial and ethnic groups are as follows: [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Disparity in Early Detection of Breast Cancer  -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Breast cancer is the most common malignancy affecting women of every race and ethnic group worldwide. In the United States, 1 in 8 women will develop breast cancer in their lifetime, although the incidence and mortality rate varies among different races. [1] Due to significant advances in early detection and available therapies, the overall mortality rate of breast cancer is declining in the United States, though not all races or ethnicities have benefited equally. [2] The incidence of late-stage diagnosis and overall mortality remains higher among certain minority groups, especially Black women. [3] Similar inequities exist at every phase of breast cancer care, from initial screening to timely follow-up and appropriate therapy completion. These substantial disparities are multifactorial, with cultural, environmental, biological, and systems-based issues contributing. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Therapy for Early-Stage Breast Cancer -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: In 2017, it is estimated that there will about 252,000 new cases of invasive breast cancer diagnosed in the United States along with 63,000 of noninvasive breast cancer (in-situ) cases. About 40,000 women are expected to die, although mortality rates have been declining. In the United States, breast cancer is the second most common cause of cancer-related deaths after lung cancer. Mortality rates are however higher in African Americans compared to whites.  The risk of developing and dying from breast cancer is lower in Asians, Hispanics, and Native Americans. [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Disparity in Early Detection of Breast Cancer  -- Issues of Concern -- Racial Disparities in Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Yet, there is a troubling reality concerning the mortality rates for women with breast cancer. While the 5-year survival rate has increased for all women over the last 40 years, a substantial racial gap persists. The incidence of breast cancer is now quite similar between non-Hispanic White and Black women in the United States, yet Black women are 42% more likely to die from the disease than any other racial group at every age, even among patients with similar staging and tumor subtypes. Not only do Black women have higher mortality rates, but they are more often first diagnosed at regional or distant stages when compared with non-Hispanic White women (45% versus 35%). [5] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Breast Cancer -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Invasive breast cancer remains the most common cancer among women worldwide, accounting for approximately 11.7% of new cases in 2020. [6] In the US, 1 in 8 women and 1 in 1000 men will develop breast cancer during their lifetime. [7] [8] [9] The incidence rate of breast cancer increases with age, from 1.5 cases per 100,000 in women aged 20 to 24 to a peak of 421.3 cases per 100,000 in women aged 75 to 79; 95% of new cases occur in women aged 40 years or older. The median age of women at the time of breast cancer diagnosis is 61 years.

retrieved_docs.title: Inflammatory Breast Cancer -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Based on the data from the surveillance, epidemiology, and end results (SEER) program of the National Cancer Institute, throughout the 1990s, IBC incidence rose, and survival improved modestly. Substantial racial differences were noted in age at diagnosis, age-specific incidence rates, and survival outcomes. [3] The incidence of IBC has remained essentially stable for nearly two decades, from 1990 to 2002. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Breast Cancer Screening in the Average-Risk Patient -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Breast cancer is the most common non-skin cancer and the second leading cause of cancer-related deaths in women. Early detection through screening can significantly reduce both morbidity and mortality. Screening recommendations distinguish between women at average and high risk for developing breast cancer, as this risk stratification guides the selection of screening methods and timing. Most major guidelines recommend starting routine mammography at age 40 for women at average risk. Although the frequency of screening—either annually or biennially—varies among guidelines, the overall trend is toward increased surveillance due to its potential to save lives.

retrieved_docs.title: Survival Analysis -- Issues of Concern -- Life Tables
retrieved_docs.content: Life tables (also known as actuarial life tables) differ from other methods of survival analysis in that the observation outputs categorize into distinct time events. [6] [7] Life tables present outputs that show whether the case occurred, whether the individual was censored, and the time to that event. [8] When examining a life table, one can determine the proportion of patients experiencing an event (e.g., dying) during which interval the outcome occurred. Life tables can also denote which individuals were censored and which were not (experienced the event in the timeframe). Within these life tables, one should assume no changes in experiencing an event at different intervals. For example, if patients with breast cancer received a second drug halfway through the treatment, they may be less likely to experience the event of dying, thus potentially invalidating results. Life tables remain useful, but in 1958, Kaplan and Meier proposed a new method that removed the pre-fixed time interval requirement. With their method, the events/outcomes of interests are assessable as they happen regardless of specific time point intervals. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Breast Ductal Carcinoma in Situ -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Overall, breast cancer is the most common cancer in women in the United States. In women aged 50 to 64 years of age, the risk of DCIS is as high as 88 per 100,000 women. [4] Today, 20% to 25% of breast cancer diagnosed in the United States is DCIS. This has increased concurrently with screening mammography, as a significant percentage of DCIS is first identified on screening mammography. In the pre-screening mammography era, less than 5% of newly diagnosed breast cancers were DCIS. A recent 2020 NCI-funded study modeled the natural history of DCIS using a combination of 2 population models and 6 submodels. They found that 36% to 100% of DCIS progressed to invasive breast cancer without surgical excision or treatment, with a mean progression time of 0.2 to 2.5 years. DCIS overdiagnosis ranged from 3.1% to 65.8%. They concluded that the majority of DCIS would progress to invasive breast cancer. However, the wide variation has to do with the heterogeneity and subtype variation within DCIS, and more studies need to be done on various subtypes. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Breast Cancer -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis of breast cancer depends on the stage. Stage 0 and Stage I both have a 100% 5-year survival rate. The 5-year survival rate of Stage II and Stage III breast cancer is about 93% and 72%, respectively. When the disease spreads systemically, its prognosis worsens dramatically. Only 22% of Stage IV breast cancer patients will survive their next 5 years. [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Cancer Screening -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: The ACS projects approximately 1.9 million newly diagnosed cancer cases and 609,360 cancer deaths in the United States in 2022. The cancer death rate increased until 1991, then steadily declined through 2017, resulting in a 29% overall decrease and approximately 2.9 million fewer deaths than if peak rates had continued. This improvement was largely due to long-term reductions in death rates for lung, colorectal, breast, and prostate cancers. However, the decline in breast and CRC cases slowed for women and stopped for prostate cancer from 2008 to 2017. In contrast, lung cancer cases in men declined more rapidly, from 3% annually between 2008 and 2013 to 5% annually between 2013 and 2017. Despite this trend, lung cancer still caused more deaths in 2017 than breast, prostate, colorectal, and brain cancers combined. Even though CRC screening has improved compared to past rates, specific populations significantly lag in screening practices, including:

retrieved_docs.title: Cancer Screening -- Function -- Breast Cancer
retrieved_docs.content: Breast cancer is the most common cancer among women and the second most common cause of cancer death in the United States. Female breast cancer incidence was on a declining trend before 2004 but has since increased at an average of 0.4% per year. After an initial incidence reduction in the early 2000s following the cessation of hormone replacement therapy, the period from 2004 to 2014 showed an increase in incidence, which may partially be attributed to the obesity epidemic.

retrieved_docs.title: Breast Cancer -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Breast cancer is the most common cancer diagnosed in women, accounting for more than 1 in 10 new cancer diagnoses annually, and is the second most common cause of cancer death among women worldwide. The risk factors for breast cancer are well established, and risk reduction plays a vital role in reducing the incidence of breast cancer. Breast cancer typically evolves silently, usually discovered on routine screening in the Western world. Without screening, breast cancer is often detected as a palpable breast mass. Surgery, radiation, chemotherapy, and immunotherapy are used in combination to treat breast cancer, depending on the stage and type of tumor. Improvements in these treatment modalities have resulted in significant improvements in overall survival and patient-reported outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Therapy for Early-Stage Breast Cancer -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Breast cancer is the most frequently diagnosed cancer in women worldwide. In the United States, it is the most common cancer (excluding skin cancer) among women. Approximately 250,000 cases of invasive breast cancer are diagnosed each year, with about 40,000 breast cancer deaths. Breast cancer deaths have been declining, likely as a result of a combination of increased awareness, early detection through screening, and modern improvement in cancer therapy over the past couple of decades. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Disparity in Early Detection of Breast Cancer  -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Breast cancer is one of the most frequently diagnosed malignancies among women globally and remains a leading cause of cancer-related mortality. Despite advancements in screening, diagnosis, and treatment, significant disparities persist in incidence, stage at diagnosis, and survival rates among different racial, ethnic, and socioeconomic groups. Black, Hispanic, Native American, and underserved women often face delayed detection, reduced access to high-quality care, and poorer outcomes. A combination of biological differences, social determinants of health, systemic inequities, and barriers to timely screening and treatment influences these disparities. Understanding the multifaceted nature of these disparities is critical to implementing targeted, equitable strategies in breast cancer care.

retrieved_docs.title: Disparity in Early Detection of Breast Cancer  -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Studies have identified strategies to reduce racial disparity in breast cancer outcomes and improve all women's clinical care. Still, the broader implementation of these existing strategies needs to take place. Furthermore, interprofessional teams must work together to innovate new policies to reduce the racial gap seen in disease-free survival rates and institute the necessary changes to achieve those goals and provide the highest quality of care to all women. [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1927
retrieved_docs.content: The female breast is the anatomical location where cancer of breast is identified, serving as the primary finding site for this malignancy. In the management of breast cancer, the female breast is also the target organ for breast‑conserving surgical procedures, specifically lumpectomy and quadrantectomy of breast, which aim to remove cancerous tissue while preserving as much normal breast tissue as possible.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2195
retrieved_docs.content: The female breast serves as the anatomical site where breast cancer manifests, with “cancer of breast” identified as a finding located in this tissue. A specific manifestation of this disease is the primary malignant neoplasm of the areola of the female breast, which is classified as a type of breast cancer. Therapeutic management of breast cancer frequently involves breast‑conserving surgical procedures performed on the female breast, such as lumpectomy and quadrantectomy, which are directly related to the organ and aim to remove malignant tissue while preserving as much normal breast tissue as possible. This interconnected knowledge underscores the relationship between the site (female breast), the disease (cancer of breast and its areolar subtype), and the standard surgical interventions (lumpectomy; quadrantectomy of breast) used in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 897
retrieved_docs.content: The female breast is the anatomical site that undergoes both diagnostic and therapeutic procedures. Breast biopsy and other diagnostic interventions are performed on the female breast to obtain tissue samples and assess pathology. In addition, the female breast is the target of surgical treatments such as lumpectomy and quadrantectomy, which are procedures used to remove localized breast lesions while preserving as much normal tissue as possible. These relationships underscore the continuum of care for breast conditions, linking diagnostic evaluation with appropriate surgical management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 1047
retrieved_docs.content: The female breast is the anatomical site where cancer of breast can arise, and this disease is classified in Arabic as سرطان الثدي. Management of cancer of breast frequently involves breast‑conserving surgical procedures such as lumpectomy and quadrantectomy of breast, which are directly related to the female breast. Recognizing the female breast as the finding site of breast cancer and understanding its associated surgical options are essential for accurate diagnosis, culturally appropriate communication, and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1218
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of breast is the English designation that classifies the Arabic term سرطان الثدي, indicating that both names refer to the same disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 896
retrieved_docs.content: The observation that a biopsy of the breast shows a malignant neoplasm is a specific instance of سرطان الثدي (breast cancer). This relationship highlights that histopathologic confirmation of malignant cells in breast tissue directly defines the presence of breast cancer, which is critical for accurate diagnosis and subsequent treatment planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 4 -- Community 2833
retrieved_docs.content: Breast: excisions are classified as a subcategory of the broader surgical procedure mastectomy, and the term mastectomy is also expressed in Arabic as استئصال ثديي.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2857
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures on breast are classified under the broader category of mastectomy, and the term mastectomy corresponds to the Arabic phrase استئصال ثديي, indicating that both refer to the surgical removal of breast tissue.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 944
retrieved_docs.content: Female breast serves as the anatomical location where cancer of breast is found, with breast cancer defined as a malignant tumor of the nipple in females (ورم خبيث في الحلمة لدى الإناث). The female breast is therefore the primary anatomic site associated with this malignant nipple tumor. Surgical management of breast cancer commonly involves procedures performed on the female breast, specifically lumpectomy and quadrantectomy of breast. These relationships underscore that the female breast is both the site of disease manifestation and the target of operative interventions aimed at removing malignant tissue.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 250
retrieved_docs.content: excision (lumpectomy/quadrantectomy) and total mastectomy with associated lymph‑node removal—providing clinicians with a structured framework for selecting appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic strategies for breast pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2128
retrieved_docs.content: CT of breast is classified as a type of تصوير الثدي الشعاعي, which denotes radiographic imaging of the breast.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 1267
retrieved_docs.content: The female breast is the anatomical site for a range of diagnostic and surgical interventions. Breast biopsy and other diagnostic procedures on breast are performed on the female breast to obtain tissue samples for evaluation, and this category specifically includes the procedure referred to in Arabic as خزعة الثدي. In addition, surgical treatments such as lumpectomy and quadrantectomy of breast are directly related to the female breast, aiming to excise localized lesions while preserving as much normal tissue as possible. Together, these diagnostic and therapeutic procedures constitute essential components of breast disease assessment and management, enabling accurate diagnosis and appropriate treatment planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2193
retrieved_docs.content: The phrase “biopsy of breast showed malignant neoplasm” is a specific outcome of a breast biopsy (خزعة الثدي) and serves as a diagnostic indicator of breast cancer (سرطان الثدي). In other words, when a breast biopsy reveals a malignant neoplasm, it confirms the presence of breast cancer. Moreover, “breast biopsy and other diagnostic procedures on breast” (خزعة الثدي) represents the broader category of breast tissue sampling and related diagnostic techniques, of which the malignant‑neoplasm finding is one particular result. This relationship highlights how breast biopsy procedures are used to detect malignant disease and how the detection of a malignant neoplasm directly links the procedure to a diagnosis of breast cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1533
retrieved_docs.content: The areola is identified as the direct site where a biopsy of a lesion is performed, and it is also recognized as a location where breast cancer may be found, highlighting its importance in diagnostic evaluation of potential breast malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 4 -- Community 2863
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures on breast and breast: excisions are both classified as subcategories of mastectomy, indicating that mastectomy serves as the parent operation that encompasses these specific breast surgical interventions.
